narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which actually stands out above the typical 3.21 average—and your LSAT score of 34, along with your plans to attend full-time from an upper-middle-class family background. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.6% probability that you would fail it. This tipped the scales just enough for your application to be rejected, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school lagged behind most peers who pass the bar. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's higher than typical and a key sign of sustained ability. Your family income bracket had a milder but still notable push toward failure, as upper-middle status sometimes correlates with other patterns in the data that challenge bar success. Compared to typical students, your full-time status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much, while your LSAT was a bit below the 36.7 average but not the main issue. Overall, the model saw your dip in later school grades as the biggest red flag outweighing your solid undergrad record. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student navigating these academics. Reapplying next cycle with those improvements could flip the prediction in your favor. You're clearly capable; it's about aligning your profile with what the model rewards. Keep pushing—you've got this.
